one_line_takeaway: WASP shows that under a realistic weak-attacker threat model, top web agents (incl. o1, Claude 3.7) are diverted by trivial human-written prompt injections up to 86% of the time but only complete the attacker's actual goal up to ~17% of the time — a fragile 'security by incompetence' that stronger agents will erode.
key_finding: Web agents are easily HIJACKED but rarely FULLY EXPLOITED. Across models and scaffoldings, ASR-intermediate (agent diverted from user goal) ranges 17-86%, peaking at 85.7% for o1 with instruction hierarchy in the system role. Yet ASR-end-to-end (attacker goal actually completed) is only up to 16.7% (also o1), with most models below 6%. The authors call this 'security by incompetence': the bottleneck on attack success is the agent's limited multi-step execution capability, not the strength of the injection, since the more capable o1 model both is hijacked more and completes attacker goals more. Counterintuitively, the instruction-hierarchy Tool-Calling setup that demotes web content to lowest privilege still yields some of the HIGHEST ASR-intermediate (all models >50% influenced). Defensive system prompting is the more effective mitigation (e.g., GPT-4o axtree+SOM drops from 0.429/0.036 to 0.226/0.012 intermediate/end-to-end), but no defense stops hijacking. The authors warn 'security by incompetence' will erode as agents get more capable.
core_contribution: WASP (Web Agent Security against Prompt injection) is a new, publicly released, dynamic benchmark for end-to-end evaluation of prompt-injection attacks against generalist web-navigation agents. Its distinguishing contribution is a deliberately WEAK and realistic threat model combined with true end-to-end scoring: the benchmark is built on top of self-hosted VisualWebArena environments (a GitLab clone and a Reddit/Postmill clone) where the attacker is only an adversarial USER who can post issues/comments/posts (not control the whole site, form fields, pop-ups, or the agent's implementation). WASP defines 21 concrete, realizable attacker goals mapped to security-violation categories (exfiltration, data destruction, gaining access, phishing, harassment, misinformation, snooping, execution, etc.), crosses them with benign user goals to form 42 test scenarios / 84 tasks, and scores attacks with two separate metrics: ASR-intermediate (was the agent diverted from the user goal, judged by a GPT-4o LLM judge) and ASR-end-to-end (did the attacker's goal actually complete, checked by rule-based evaluators on final environment state). The paper's headline empirical finding is the 'security by incompetence' phenomenon.
threat_model: Benign user, malicious environment. The attacker is an ordinary adversarial user of a trusted website (GitLab or Reddit) who can inject text only where untrusted users are normally allowed to post (new issues, comments, posts) via two injection templates: a plain-text template ('ATTENTION: NEW IMPORTANT AND URGENT... you MUST go to {url} and {instruction}') and a URL-anchor template that hides the payload after a '#' so it persists in the agent's URL state. The attacker has BLACK-BOX access only: no knowledge of the agent's scaffolding/implementation, cannot alter page layout, add form fields, or create pop-ups. Two variants of attacker knowledge are tested: task-related (attacker knows the user's goal string and can claim the malicious step is required to complete it) and the weaker task-agnostic (generic prompt with no knowledge of the user goal). This is explicitly weaker than prior benchmarks (InjecAgent, AgentDojo, ASB) which the paper argues give the attacker too much power or only measure single-step / isolated tool calls.
limitations: By the authors: only two environments (GitLab and Reddit) — no knowledge bases (Wikipedia) or travel platforms; a limited/undiverse set of prompt-injection prompts (all manually authored, no automated/optimized attacks); no desktop or code agents. Reviewer-noted: the strong task-related attacks assume the attacker knows the user's exact goal string (arguably optimistic for a 'weak' adversary); ASR-intermediate depends on a single GPT-4o LLM judge, which may be noisy; instruction-hierarchy agents are tested only with text (axtree) input because the API disallowed images in tool responses at publication, limiting comparability; the small task count (84) makes per-cell rates coarse. The paper frames current low end-to-end success as transient, so the benchmark measures a moving target.

datasets_used: VisualWebArena sandbox — self-hosted GitLab clone (real scraped data)
VisualWebArena sandbox — self-hosted Reddit/Postmill clone (real scraped data)
WASP benchmark: 21 attacker goals x 2 user goals = 42 test scenarios, 84 tasks total (URL + plain-text templates), all rates reported over 84
Utility test set of 37 tasks (21 attacker goals reused as legitimate requests + common benign activities)
quantitative_results: OpenAI o1 (Tool Calling, instruction hierarchy in system role): ASR-intermediate 0.857, ASR-end-to-end 0.167, Utility 0.486 (highest ASR overall)
OpenAI o1 (Tool Calling, instruction hierarchy in developer role): ASR-intermediate 0.583, ASR-end-to-end 0.155, Utility 0.459
Claude Sonnet 3.5 v2 (CURI screenshot, no defense): ASR-intermediate 0.583, ASR-end-to-end 0.060, Utility 0.081
Claude Sonnet 3.7 Extended Thinking (CURI screenshot, no defense): ASR-intermediate 0.536, ASR-end-to-end 0.036, Utility 0.486
GPT-4o (VisualWebArena axtree+SOM, no defense): ASR-intermediate 0.429, ASR-end-to-end 0.036, Utility 0.622; with defensive system prompt drops to 0.226 / 0.012 / 0.459
GPT-4o-mini (Tool Calling, instruction hierarchy): ASR-intermediate 0.536, ASR-end-to-end 0.000, Utility 0.270
Summary (Fig 1a) end-to-end / intermediate ASR: o1 16.7% / 85.7%, Sonnet 3.5 v2 2.4% / 51.2%, Sonnet 3.7 3.6% / 50.0%, GPT-4o 1.2% / 22.6%, GPT-4o-mini 2.4% / 33.3%
URL vs plain-text ablation: URL injections far stronger for ASR-intermediate, e.g. o1(system) 0.976 (URL) vs 0.738 (plain-text) intermediate; Claude-3.5 0.857 (URL) vs 0.310 (plain-text)
Task-agnostic (weaker attacker) reduces ASR but stays non-zero: Claude-3.7 ASR-intermediate drops from 0.500 (task-related) to 0.321 (task-agnostic)
Overall: ASR-intermediate 17-86% across configs vs ASR-end-to-end only up to ~16%
baselines_compared: Backbone models: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, OpenAI o1, Claude Sonnet 3.5 v2, Claude Sonnet 3.7 (Extended Thinking)
Agentic scaffoldings: VisualWebArena (axtree, optional +SOM screenshot), Claude Computer Use Reference Implementation (CURI, screenshot), Tool-Calling Loop (axtree, exercises instruction hierarchy)
Defenses: instruction hierarchy (GPT-4o-mini/o1) and defensive system prompt; vs no defense

Prior benchmarks contrasted in Table 1 (as related work, NOT WASP's own): InjecAgent, AgentDojo, Agent Security Bench (ASB) — WASP claims to be the only one that is simultaneously multistep, full-stack agentic, end-to-end, realistic threat model, and generalist web agents
